course_description:
This course introduces students to web design, user experience (UX), web-based user interface design (UI), and front-end development (FED). Students learn to design and develop human-centred websites through integrated study of design principles, accessibility, ethical practice, and modern development techniques. The course emphasises continuous practice, critical judgement, responsible tool use (including AI tools), and practice-based research to support professional capability in contemporary web design and development.

This course treats web design and development as a practiced capability, not a body of knowledge, developed through structured, continuous engagement that builds both technical competence and professional resilience: capability development through continuous, scaffolded practice.

This course provides a practice-led introduction to User Experience (UX), User Interface (UI), and Front-End Development (FED). You learn by designing, building, testing, and refining real websites across the semester using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

A central feature of the course is ongoing practice and iterative learning. Rather than memorising tools or rules, you develop capability through a structured learning loop: analysing client briefs and user data, translating insights into design decisions, implementing those decisions in code, testing your work, and refining it. This repeated cycle builds technical skill alongside professional judgement.

Applied classes play an essential role in this process. They are assessment-aligned and designed to guide you through key stages of each task. In these sessions, you practise problem-solving, receive feedback, clarify expectations, and progressively develop your work. Assessment is therefore integrated into learning and supports growth over time, rather than evaluating a single final product.

The course emphasises responsive, accessible, and ethically responsible design. You apply UX/UI principles, accessibility standards, and culturally respectful practices in both design and implementation.

You also develop responsible AI decision-making capability. You may choose to use AI tools, limit their use, or deliberately not use them. What is assessed is your judgement - how you verify outputs, maintain authorship, and demonstrate critical and ethical reasoning in an AI-present environment.

By the end of the course, you will be able to design and implement accessible, human-centred websites, use professional development workflows, and clearly justify your design and technical decisions using evidence and structured reasoning.

As a Master’s-level (AQF-9) course, this subject includes a research component. Students use user research, design theory, accessibility standards, and ethical frameworks to inform their decisions, and clearly justify their reasoning through annotated artefacts and reflection.

Changes in response to student feedback

The number of assessment tasks has been reduced to 3 in total.

learning_outcomes.description: Web Design (including accessibility): Analyse client goals and user research to design human-centred website structures and interfaces, and justify design decisions using UX/UI theory, accessibility standards, and ethical considerations. [THIS LEARNING OBJECTIVE CONTRIBUTES TO AQF9 RESEARCH FOR MASTERS PROGRAMS]

Web Development (HTML, CSS, JavaScript): Develop, implement, and evaluate responsive, accessible web pages using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, following contemporary front-end development practices and coding standards.

Tools for Design and Development: Employ industry-standard tools, including design software, development environments, version control, and verification tools, to support, improve, and document design and development processes.

Ethics in Web Design and Development: Identify, evaluate, and apply ethical principles to design and development decisions, demonstrating awareness of cultural, social, accessibility, and data-related impacts in professional web practice.

Responsible Use or Non-use of AI: Direct, evaluate, and validate decisions about AI-assisted contributions within design and development workflows, while maintaining human judgement, authorship, and academic or professional integrity.

assessment_summary.due_date: Progress Check-in A1.1: During your allocated applied class in Week 4 Mon - Week 4 Fri; Progress Check-in A1.2: During your allocated applied class in Week 7 Mon - Week 7 Fri; Progress Check-in A1.3: During your allocated applied class in Week 9 Mon - Week 9 Fri; Progress Check-in A1.4: During your allocated applied class in Week 12 Mon - Week 12 Fri

assessment_details.title: A1 Individual Progress Check-ins: Capability-Referenced, Guided Professional Practice
assessment_details.category: Computer Code, Notebook/ Logbook, Practical/ Demonstration
assessment_details.due_date: Progress Check-in A1.1: During your allocated applied class in Week 4 Mon - Week 4 Fri Progress Check-in A1.2: During your allocated applied class in Week 7 Mon - Week 7 Fri Progress Check-in A1.3: During your allocated applied class in Week 9 Mon - Week 9 Fri Progress Check-in A1.4: During your allocated applied class in Week 12 Mon - Week 12 Fri
assessment_details.task_description: Use of AI is RESTRICTED in this Assessment Task This assessment task is completed in person as an identity-verified progress check-in. Students are not permitted to use AI tools to generate answers or complete assessment requirements during the check-in . However, because responsible and transparent use of AI tools is a core learning focus of the course, students may discuss, explain, or demonstrate prior use (or non-use) of AI tools as part of the guided professional conversation. This may include showing examples of how AI tools were used during their development process, explaining verification steps, or reflecting on limitations, risks, and decision-making. In some cases, the teaching team member may invite a student to trial or explore an AI tool during the discussion for learning purposes. Any such use occurs under supervision and forms part of the assessment of professional judgement, not task completion. Any unauthorised or deceptive use of AI tools during the check-in , including attempting to use AI to generate responses in place of explanation or reasoning, may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. What is this task? The A1 Individual Progress Check-ins are structured one-on-one conversations with a member of the teaching team. Their purpose is to support your learning by reviewing your current progress, understanding, and decision-making at key points across the semester. In each check-in, you will discuss the work you have completed to date and explain how and why you have made particular design and development decisions appropriate to the current stage of the course. These conversations are not only about what is working, but also about identifying challenges, reflecting on feedback, and planning next steps. The check-ins are designed to help you develop professional habits of practice, including explaining your work clearly, exercising judgement about priorities and trade-offs, and progressively building independence over time. How it is assessed Across the semester, you will be assessed on four capabilities: Professional reasoning and decision-making Engagement in guided professional dialogue Progressive development and professional autonomy Responsible AI decision-making (or justified non-use) Each capability is assessed as: Developing (Level 1), Competent (Level 2), or Advanced (Level 3). Individual Progress Review A1.1 is timed to provide feedback on your progress prior to the Semester 1, 2026 Census (31 March, 2026). Your final A1 grade is determined using a standards-based translation process that: considers capability demonstrated across the semester, and requires demonstration of minimum capability by the final check-in. A detailed task sheet and grading criteria (including the standards-based translation process) for this assessment item is available in Blackboard in the Assessment Folder. It is important that you read both of these documents to understand all of the requirements of this assessment item. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The progress check-ins are in-person interactive oral assessment during your allocated applied class. These are not artefact submissions but identity-verified, time-bound professional conversations that require attendance, preparation, and active engagement at a specific point in the semester.
Each check-in is assessed during the applied class by a member of the teaching team. Your identity will be verified during the check-in, so please bring your student card.
In accordance with UQ Assessment Policy, your progress check-in will be video recorded.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions for this assessment item are limited to a maximum of 14 days .
This assessment is intentionally designed as part of a progressive sequence , where each check-in aligns with weekly learning activities and directly supports subsequent assessment items. Extensions beyond 14 days would compromise the alignment between assessment, feedback, and later tasks.
The extension limit ensures that formative feedback remains timely and meaningful, allowing students to progress effectively through the assessment sequence, and is consistent with professional and industry expectations around scheduled progress reviews.
This has been approved by the Associate Dean (Academic).
If you have documented grounds for being absent from a scheduled progress check-in (for example, approved medical, compassionate, or accessibility-related reasons), you should apply for an extension (following the procedure identified above) as soon as possible.
Where appropriate documentation is provided, reasonable accommodation will be arranged. For this assessment item, this typically involves scheduling an alternative time to complete the progress check-in as an in-person or live interactive conversation with the course coordinator or a nominated member of the teaching team.
Alternative arrangements are intended to ensure equitable access to assessment while preserving the identity-verified, interactive nature of the task. They are arranged on a case-by-case basis and must take place within the approved extension timeframe.
Students who do not attend a scheduled progress check-in and do not have approved grounds will be recorded as having not completed that check-in, which may affect the maximum grade achievable for A1.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
The A1 Individual Progress Check-ins take place during scheduled applied classes. As a result, late submission is not possible. A 100% late penalty with no grace period applies to any Progress Check-in not completed at the scheduled time. In the context of this assessment, a late penalty is recorded as a missed check-in , which may limit the maximum grade achievable for A1 and/or affect the minimum capability requirement for the course. This has been approved by the Associate Dean (Academic).

assessment_details.title: A2 Research and Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: Use of AI tools is PERMITTED in this assessment task. This course explicitly develops students’ capability for responsible AI decision-making as part of professional web design and development practice. Students may choose to use AI tools, to limit or constrain their use, or to deliberately not use AI tools, provided that this decision is made thoughtfully, transparently, and in alignment with the course Responsible AI Decision-Making Capability Framework . Where AI tools are used, students are expected to: maintain human judgement, authorship, and decision-making authority, critically evaluate and verify AI-generated outputs, use AI tools to support learning and professional reasoning rather than replace them, and transparently acknowledge and reflect on their use as required by the assessment task. Responsible AI decision-making (including justified use, selective use, or deliberate non-use) forms part of the assessment expectations. What is this task? A2: Research and Proposal is an integrated assessment task where you investigate a design problem, analyse research findings, and develop a clear proposal for the website you will build in A3. In this task, you will respond to the Connected Communities Initiative (CCI) client brief , using research to inform design requirements and proposing a website that meets the client’s goals, values, and constraints. You will submit your work as a single, integrated website , where: research activities and findings are documented, insights are synthesised into design requirements and principles, and a structured design proposal is presented and justified. This task brings together research, design thinking, and early implementation in one coherent artefact. What do I need to produce? You will produce a deployed website that integrates research and proposal content. Specifically, you must submit: A website deployed to UQCloud A zip file containing the website source files A GitHub repository, used to verify development progress if needed Within the website, you will present: your research process and key findings, clear synthesis of those findings into design requirements, a structured design proposal for your website, partially implemented design components (e.g. UI elements demonstrated within style guide or proposal pages), all research, synthesis, and proposal content must clearly demonstrate how your design responds to the CCI client brief, and a reflection on your use or non-use of AI tools following the course requirements on Responsible AI Use . Important: How this assessment is supported in class The applied class activities for this course have been specifically designed to guide you through the completion of this assessment. These activities provide detailed guidance on what is expected and how to approach each component of the task. If you are unable to attend a class, it is essential that you engage with the activities independently, as they form a core part of the learning required to complete this assessment successfully. More information about the requirements for this assessment item are provided in an Assessment Task Sheet that will be available in the course Blackboard site. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You will submit a functional website (following the requirements detailed in the task sheet for this assessment item) by:
deploying your website to your UQCloud zone uploading a zip of all website files to Blackboard committing and pushing your code to your GitHub repository
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: A3 Website Development
assessment_details.task_description: Use of AI tools is PERMITTED in this assessment task. This course explicitly develops students’ capability for responsible AI decision-making as part of professional web design and development practice. Students may choose to use AI tools, to limit or constrain their use, or to deliberately not use AI tools, provided that this decision is made thoughtfully, transparently, and in alignment with the course Responsible AI Decision-Making Capability Framework . Where AI tools are used, students are expected to: maintain human judgement, authorship, and decision-making authority, critically evaluate and verify AI-generated outputs, use AI tools to support learning and professional reasoning rather than replace them, and transparently acknowledge and reflect on their use as required by the assessment task. Responsible AI decision-making (including justified use, selective use, or deliberate non-use) forms part of the assessment expectations. What is this task? A3: Website Development is your opportunity to turn your A2 decisions into a working website. You will implement the website structure, visual and interaction design, and key functionality using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript , while integrating accessibility and ethical considerations in practice. This task recognises that implementation reveals constraints. You may need to adapt parts of your A2 proposal, but you must justify any variations and show how your final build still aligns with your earlier reasoning. What do I need to produce? You will produce and submit a 7-page website deployed online (and committed to version control), implemented with component-based CSS and modular JavaScript , and incorporating required API interactions. Your website must include the following pages: Landing page : index.html About page : a text-heavy page demonstrating strong layout, typography, readability, hierarchy, and scanning support Form page : includes a form that submits data to the course API using JavaScript API content page : retrieves content from the course API using JavaScript and dynamically renders it into the page with appropriate styling Extension page : an additional page of your choice that showcases your skills (e.g., a component demo page, interactive feature, accessibility enhancement, or design variation) Variations page : a page that details any variations from your A2 Research and Proposal to your final implementation AI Reflection page : a page that contains your reflection on AI use or non-use in accordance with the course requirements on Responsible AI Use . In addition, across the website you must demonstrate: Component-based CSS (reusable classes/components rather than page-specific styling only) Modular JavaScript (separation of concerns; reusable functions/modules rather than one large script file) Important: How this assessment is supported in class The applied class activities for this course have been specifically designed to guide you through the completion of this assessment. These activities provide detailed guidance on what is expected and how to approach each component of the task. If you are unable to attend a class, it is essential that you engage with the activities independently, as they form a core part of the learning required to complete this assessment successfully. More information about the requirements for this assessment item are provided in an Assessment Task Sheet that will be available in the course Blackboard site. Read full task description Hide full task description

The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Note that extensions on an assessment item due in the exam period may lead to an INC grade being entered for the course.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13
learning_activities.topic: Lectures | Weeks 1 to 13 Lectures in this course provide the conceptual foundations and theoretical frameworks for key topics in User Experience Design (UX), User Interface Design (UI), and Frontend Development (FED). Rather than functioning as extended presentations, lectures are designed as guided learning sessions that build understanding through preparation, discussion, and application. Short preparatory videos or podcasts are generally provided before each lecture to introduce core concepts, terminology, and examples. These materials allow you to engage with the foundational content in advance, so that scheduled lecture time can be used for discussion, clarification, and short structured activities that deepen understanding. Key features of lectures include: Conceptual foundations: Lectures introduce and contextualise essential theories and principles, including usability, accessibility, ethical design, and interaction design concepts that underpin effective web design and development. Active engagement: Scheduled lecture time is used for discussion, reflection, and small activities that help you interrogate ideas, connect theory to practice, and prepare for applied work. Scaffolded learning: Lecture topics are carefully sequenced to build progressively across the semester, supporting increasing independence and complexity in applied and assessment work. Industry relevance: Concepts and examples are grounded in contemporary professional practice, helping you develop transferable understanding rather than tool-specific knowledge. Lectures provide the direction and framing for learning in the course, while deeper understanding is developed through Applied Classes, independent activities, and assessment tasks. Attendance is encouraged, and all preparatory materials, recordings, and resources are made available for review.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13
learning_activities.topic: Applied Classes | Weeks 1 to 13 Applied Classes are the practical core of this course. They are structured, hands-on sessions where you apply lecture concepts directly to design and development work, supported by guided activities and feedback from the teaching team. These classes are designed to reflect professional web design and development practice. Rather than following step-by-step instructions to produce a fixed outcome, you will engage in iterative design, coding, testing, debugging, and refinement. The focus is on developing understanding, judgement, and professional habits through practice. Key features of Applied Classes include: Applied learning: Activities are closely aligned with weekly lecture content, enabling you to put theory into practice immediately through design and development tasks. Progressive expectations: Activities are sequenced across the semester to move from guided practice toward greater independence, mirroring the increasing expectations across assessment tasks. Feedback and support: Applied Classes provide opportunities for formative feedback, discussion, and clarification, helping you identify issues early and refine your approach before assessment submission. Assessment alignment" Applied Class activities are intentionally designed to support the completion of assessment tasks. Work completed in these sessions contributes directly to your progress in A1, A2, and A3. Professional practice habits: You are encouraged to explain your decisions, respond to feedback, reflect on challenges, and make responsible choices about tools and workflows, including the use or non-use of AI tools. To benefit fully from Applied Classes, you are expected to come prepared, actively engage with the activities, and use feedback to inform your ongoing work. If you are unable to attend a scheduled class, equivalent activities and resources will be provided, and you are responsible for completing them independently.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: EECS School Guidelines for Students Learning activities
